summary: My family came to the US from England in 1982 when I was four, but I never really felt a strong connection to England. We visited England again when I was 10, which made me feel like a citizen of the world, but I always felt more American than British. I even did part of my MBA in London, yet I felt like a tourist there just like my classmates. England is the only place my parents took us on vacation, and interestingly, we never made it to Scotland.

I remember coming to the US on a big TWA jet with my family when I was four years old. The only vivid memory I have from that flight is holding my dad's thumb tightly as the plane took off. That flight marked the beginning of my life in America, which I now fully identify with after 35 years.

Although my family is from England, we never made it to Scotland on any of our trips. I often joke that my parents never took us on vacations more than a few hours from home because they considered any travel away from England as traveling. So Scotland remains a place we never visited, even though it's part of the UK like England.

After we moved to the US, my family lived briefly in Maryland before moving on to Ohio and then settling in Iowa. Maryland was one of the first places we lived in America, but it was only a short stop in our journey. I don't have many strong memories tied to Maryland compared to where I ended up calling home.

Ohio was another place my family lived briefly after Maryland and before settling in Iowa. Like Maryland, it was part of our early years in the US, but it didn't feel like home to me. My parents kept moving until they found a place in Iowa where we stayed longer and where I consider myself from.

After living briefly in Maryland and Ohio, my parents finally settled us in Iowa, which is where I really consider myself from. It's a fairly small hometown, and growing up there shaped a lot of my identity. Even though I eventually moved away, Iowa is where I feel rooted and where my family established itself in the US.

I have lived in New York City for fourteen years now, far away from my small hometown in Iowa. Moving to New York was a big change, but it felt natural since I always assumed I would move away from where I grew up. New York has been my home for a significant part of my adult life and career.
